steps.title: Identify what the blank must do
steps.content: The passage claims that a general measure of economic policy uncertainty may not fully reflect uncertainty about specific areas of policy. The blank must supply UK data that illustrates this divergence — a case where the general measure and a specific-area measure move in noticeably different directions or magnitudes.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice using the graph data
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'aligned closely with uncertainty about tax and public spending policy in 2005 but differed from uncertainty about tax and public spending policy by a large amount in 2009.': The graph shows general uncertainty (~90) and tax/spending uncertainty (~100) were similar in 2005, which is accurate, but in 2009 both values were close (~80 and ~75), so the claimed large difference does not exist. ✗
  • Choice B — 'was substantially lower than uncertainty about tax and public spending policy each year from 2005 to 2010.': The graph shows general uncertainty was actually higher than tax/spending uncertainty in 2006, 2007, and 2009, so "each year" is false. ✗
  • Choice C — 'reached its highest level between 2005 and 2010 in the same year that uncertainty about trade policy and tax and public spending policy reached their lowest levels.': General uncertainty peaked in 2010, but tax/spending uncertainty also peaked in 2010, not reached its lowest level. ✗
  • Choice D — 'was substantially lower than uncertainty about trade policy in 2005 and substantially higher than uncertainty about trade policy in 2010.': The graph shows general uncertainty (~90) was well below trade uncertainty (~160) in 2005, and general uncertainty (~120) was well above trade uncertainty (~70) in 2010 — a clear reversal that directly illustrates the divergence. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
